propagation: Divide the rhizome, or detach the adventitious plantlets that form on older leaf tips and attach them elsewhere.
careSummary: Java Fern is one of the most forgiving aquarium plants available: it grows in low light, needs no CO2 or substrate, and is ignored by nearly all livestock. Tie or glue the rhizome to stone or driftwood — burying it in substrate will cause it to rot.

faqs.question: Why is my Java Fern turning black or brown?
faqs.answer: Small black spots near the leaf edges are usually spores or baby plantlets forming, which is normal. Large spreading black patches typically mean the rhizome was buried in substrate or the plant is adjusting to new water; trim affected leaves and make sure the rhizome sits on top of hardscape, fully exposed.

faqs.question: Is Java Fern a good plant for a betta tank?
faqs.answer: Yes — it is one of the best. Its broad leaves give bettas resting spots near the surface, it thrives in the low-flow, low-light conditions bettas prefer, and bettas leave it alone.

faqs.question: Will Java Fern survive with goldfish or African cichlids?
faqs.answer: It is one of the rare plants that will. The leaves are tough and bitter, so even dedicated plant-destroyers usually take one taste and move on, and because the rhizome is tied to rock or wood, diggers cannot uproot it. Expect a few cosmetic nibble marks rather than destruction.

propagation: Cut the rhizome into sections with at least three leaves each and reattach the divisions to hardscape.
careSummary: Anubias Nana is a slow-growing epiphyte with thick, dark, nearly indestructible leaves. It tolerates low light, needs no CO2, and attaches readily to stone and driftwood — keep the rhizome above the substrate and it will run for years with almost no care.

faqs.question: Why does my Anubias get algae on its leaves?
faqs.answer: Because Anubias grows so slowly, its leaves stay in place long enough for algae to settle, especially under strong light. Place it in shaded spots, keep light periods to about 7–8 hours, and let amano shrimp or nerite snails graze the leaves clean.

faqs.question: Can Anubias flower underwater?
faqs.answer: Yes. Healthy Anubias regularly produces a white, calla-lily-like flower fully submerged. It does not indicate a problem — enjoy it; the flower lasts a few weeks before melting back.

faqs.question: What is Anubias rot and can the plant be saved?
faqs.answer: Rot announces itself as a stretch of rhizome going black and mushy, often spreading from a damaged spot. Cut back to firm green tissue with sterilized scissors, discard every soft piece, and reattach the clean sections where water circulation is stronger. Caught early, the surviving rhizome typically regrows.

faqs.question: Can Anubias survive with African cichlids or goldfish?
faqs.answer: Yes — it is one of the very few plants that can. The thick, leathery leaves are too tough and unpalatable for grazers to bother with, and since the rhizome attaches to rock or wood, diggers cannot uproot it. Java Fern is about its only company in that role.

propagation: Cut the creeping rhizome into sections and attach each piece to stone or wood.
careSummary: Bucephalandra is a slow-growing Borneo epiphyte prized for its iridescent blue-green sheen and compact size. Care is nearly identical to Anubias: low light, no CO2 required, rhizome attached to hardscape — though CO2 and gentle flow deepen its color and speed up growth.

faqs.question: Why is my Bucephalandra melting after planting?
faqs.answer: Buce grown emersed (above water) often sheds its old leaves when first submerged. Leave the rhizome attached and undisturbed — new submerged-form leaves will emerge within a few weeks as long as the rhizome stays firm.

faqs.question: Does Bucephalandra need CO2?
faqs.answer: No. It grows reliably in low-tech tanks without injection, just slowly. CO2 mainly improves leaf color and density rather than being a survival requirement.

propagation: Trim and replant — any fragment containing a few fronds will grow into a new patch.
careSummary: Java Moss is the workhorse of aquarium mosses: it survives almost any light, temperature, and water chemistry, and anchors itself to stone, wood, and mesh. It is the standard choice for shrimp tanks and fry cover, and the easiest way to age a hardscape instantly. Blast detritus out of established pads with a turkey baster during water changes — dense moss traps mulm and browns from the inside if it is never cleared.

faqs.question: How do I attach Java Moss to hardscape?
faqs.answer: Spread a thin layer over the surface and hold it down with cotton thread, fishing line, or a dab of cyanoacrylate gel glue. It anchors itself with rhizoids within a few weeks; thin layers attach faster and look cleaner than thick clumps.

faqs.question: Why is my Java Moss turning brown in the middle?
faqs.answer: Dense clumps shade their own interior, and the starved inner growth browns while the outside stays green. Trim it back regularly so light and flow reach the base, and the patch will stay green throughout.

propagation: Divide a patch and re-tie the fragments; trimmed cuttings grow wherever they are anchored.
careSummary: Christmas Moss grows in neat, triangular fronds that overlap like fir branches, making it the tidier, more sculptural alternative to Java Moss. It grows slower and appreciates cooler water and a little flow, and it is the classic moss for tree-style aquascapes and moss walls. Give mature pads a routine puff from a turkey baster to flush trapped debris; mulm accumulating deep in the fronds is a common cause of browning from within.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Christmas Moss and Java Moss?
faqs.answer: Christmas Moss grows flatter and more structured, with regular side branches that give it a layered, frond-like look, while Java Moss grows in an irregular tangle. Christmas Moss is slightly more demanding — it colors best below about 78°F with some flow — but rewards you with a much neater appearance.

faqs.question: Why is my Christmas Moss growing stringy instead of bushy?
faqs.answer: Stringy, leggy growth means it is reaching for light. Increase intensity moderately or move the moss higher, and trim it back — frequent light trims are what produce the dense, layered pads the species is known for.

propagation: Cut runners or split a mat into plugs and replant them a couple of inches apart; plugs knit together into a carpet.
careSummary: Monte Carlo is the most achievable true carpeting plant: round, bright-green leaves that creep along the substrate and form a dense lawn. It carpets fastest with CO2 and medium light, but unlike its lookalike dwarf baby tears, it can still form a respectable carpet in a low-tech tank given patience and good substrate.

faqs.question: Can Monte Carlo carpet without CO2?
faqs.answer: Yes, with caveats: growth is several times slower, it carpets thinner, and it needs a nutrient-rich substrate and at least solid medium light to stay compact. Plant plugs densely from the start and expect three months or more for coverage instead of weeks.

faqs.question: Why is my Monte Carlo carpet detaching and floating up?
faqs.answer: Carpets lift when the mat grows too thick — the lower layer dies back in its own shade and loses grip. Mow the carpet down to about half an inch every few weeks and press loose sections back in so the runners re-anchor.

faqs.question: Why is my Monte Carlo growing up instead of out?
faqs.answer: Upright, leggy stems mean the plant is reaching for light it is not getting — under sufficient intensity it hugs the substrate and creeps sideways. Raise the light's output or move the fixture closer, then trim off the vertical growth so the new runners restart low and horizontal.

propagation: Spreads by runners; separate a clump into small tufts and plant them in a grid to seed a lawn.
careSummary: Dwarf Hairgrass forms the classic grassy lawn of iwagumi layouts, spreading by runners into a field of fine green blades. Give it strong-medium light, a fine substrate its runners can push through, and ideally CO2 — then trim it short early on to force dense, low growth.

faqs.question: How do I plant Dwarf Hairgrass so it actually spreads?
faqs.answer: Split the pot into a dozen or more small tufts and plant them in a grid about an inch apart, pushing the roots in and keeping the crowns at substrate level. Small, well-spaced tufts send runners far faster than a few large clumps.

faqs.question: Should I trim Dwarf Hairgrass?
faqs.answer: Yes — counterintuitively, cutting it down to about an inch shortly after planting redirects energy into runners instead of blade height, which is what fills the carpet in. After it covers, an occasional mow keeps the lawn dense and prevents the lower layer from browning.

faqs.question: Can I dry-start a Dwarf Hairgrass carpet?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it is one of the most reliable routes to a dense lawn. Plant your tufts in damp — not flooded — substrate, seal the tank to hold humidity, and let the grass run emersed for several weeks with unlimited atmospheric CO2 before filling; the established carpet barely notices the flood. Use two to three inches of substrate so the runners have depth to travel.

propagation: Trim the tops and replant them beside the parent; cut stems re-root quickly and bush outward.
careSummary: Staurogyne repens is a compact, slow-growing bush plant that stays under a few inches — the easiest path to a structured green foreground without committing to a true carpet. It tolerates low-tech setups, though medium light and CO2 keep it tight and creeping rather than upright.

faqs.question: Is Staurogyne repens a carpeting plant?
faqs.answer: Not exactly — it forms low, dense bushes rather than a uniform lawn. Planted in a row along the hardscape line and trimmed flat, it reads like a hedge or transition zone, which is how most aquascapers use it.

faqs.question: Why is my Staurogyne growing tall and leggy?
faqs.answer: Insufficient light makes it stretch vertically. Under solid medium light it hugs the substrate; trim the leggy tops, replant them, and the cut stems will branch into a denser, lower shape.

propagation: Spreads aggressively by runners; snip plantlets off the runner and replant, or let it colonize on its own.
careSummary: Dwarf Sagittaria is the low-tech answer to a grassy foreground: tougher than hairgrass, happy without CO2, and tolerant of hard, alkaline water. It spreads by runners into a slightly taller, wilder lawn — expect it to wander wherever the substrate is open.

faqs.question: Why did my Dwarf Sagittaria grow taller than expected?
faqs.answer: Under lower light it stretches to 6 inches or more, blurring the line between foreground and midground. Stronger light keeps it shorter; alternatively, embrace the height and use it as a midground transition plant.

faqs.question: Does Dwarf Sagittaria need root tabs?
faqs.answer: It is a root feeder, so in inert sand or gravel a root tab every few months makes a visible difference. In nutrient-rich aquasoil it needs nothing extra.

propagation: Produces daughter plants on short runners around the base; separate and replant them once they have a few leaves.
careSummary: Cryptocoryne wendtii is the definitive low-light midground plant, forming slow, dense rosettes in green, bronze, or red depending on the variety and light. It famously melts when first planted, then regrows adapted leaves — patience in the first month is the whole secret.

faqs.question: Why did my Crypt melt right after planting?
faqs.answer: Crypt melt is the species' standard response to changed conditions: it sheds its old leaves and regrows ones suited to your water. Leave the roots undisturbed, keep the tank stable, and new growth typically appears within two to four weeks.

faqs.question: Do Cryptocorynes need root tabs?
faqs.answer: They are heavy root feeders, so in inert substrates a root tab near the rosette every two to three months noticeably improves leaf size and color. In aquasoil they are largely self-sufficient.

propagation: Sends runners that pop up daughter plants nearby; separate them once established or let it form a colony.
careSummary: Crypt Balansae brings height to a low-tech tank with long, ruffled, strap-like leaves that sway in the current. It is one of the few dramatic background plants that thrives in low light and hard water without CO2, and like all crypts it rewards being left alone.

faqs.question: How long does Crypt Balansae take to establish?
faqs.answer: Expect an initial melt and a slow first one to two months while it roots. Once settled it steadily sends up leaves a foot or more long and spreads into an attractive colony — it is a plant you buy for month three, not week one.

faqs.question: Is Crypt Balansae good for hard water?
faqs.answer: Yes — it actually prefers harder, slightly alkaline water, a legacy of the limestone streams it comes from. It is one of the best background choices for tanks where soft-water stem plants struggle.

propagation: Sends up a long flower stalk bearing plantlets; once each plantlet has roots and a few leaves, cut it free and plant it.
careSummary: The Amazon Sword is the classic centerpiece plant: a big, hardy rosette that anchors the back of a community tank. It is an exceptionally heavy root feeder — plant it deep in substrate with root tabs and it will reward you with leaves that can fill a 20-inch-tall tank.

faqs.question: Why are my Amazon Sword's leaves turning yellow or translucent?
faqs.answer: Most often it is root-zone nutrient hunger — swords exhaust inert substrates quickly. Push a root tab or two near the crown every two to three months; new leaves should come in firm and green. Older emersed-grown leaves also die back naturally after planting as submerged leaves replace them.

faqs.question: Is an Amazon Sword too big for a 10-gallon tank?
faqs.answer: Eventually, yes. A healthy sword reaches 16–20 inches and will dominate a small tank within a year. For tanks under 20 gallons, a chain sword or Crypt Wendtii fills the same role at an appropriate scale.

propagation: Spreads rapidly by runners along the substrate; snip daughter plants free once rooted, or let it form a curtain.
careSummary: Jungle Val grows ribbon leaves a yard long that bend and flow across the surface, creating the jungle-stream canopy look with zero technology: low light, no CO2, hard water welcome. Give it space — a happy Val colony will run the length of the tank within months.

faqs.question: Why did my Vallisneria melt when I started CO2 or liquid carbon?
faqs.answer: Vals are unusually sensitive to glutaraldehyde-based liquid carbon products, which can melt them outright — avoid dosing those near Vals. Pressurized CO2 itself is tolerated, though Vals rarely need it; sudden chemistry swings are the usual culprit for melt.

faqs.question: How do I stop Jungle Val from taking over the tank?
faqs.answer: Pull runners monthly where you do not want new plants, and trim leaves diagonally near the surface. A root barrier or a band of rock between substrate zones also works — left alone, a colony will happily annex the midground.

faqs.question: How deep should I plant Jungle Val?
faqs.answer: Bury only the roots and leave the pale crown — the point where the leaves fan out — sitting just above the substrate. A Val that keeps floating free or melts away at the base was almost certainly planted too deep; reset it shallower and it usually bounces back.

propagation: The bulb produces offshoot plantlets and the mother plant can be divided; floating leaves left to flower may also set seed.
careSummary: Red Tiger Lotus erupts from a bulb into a fountain of burgundy, tiger-streaked leaves — the easiest deep-red focal plant in the hobby, needing no CO2 to color up. Keep it pruned to stay a compact midground bush, or let a few lily pads run to the surface for a wilder look.

faqs.question: How do I plant a tiger lotus bulb?
faqs.answer: Rest the bulb on top of the substrate or bury it no more than halfway, sprout-side up — fully buried bulbs rot. Once leaves and roots establish, the plant anchors itself; root tabs nearby fuel its considerable appetite.

faqs.question: How do I keep my tiger lotus small and red instead of sending pads to the surface?
faqs.answer: Pinch off any stem that races upward as soon as it appears — surface pads signal the plant to put energy into floating growth. Consistent pruning of climbers keeps it a dense, low rosette of red submerged leaves.

faqs.question: Why isn't my tiger lotus bulb sprouting?
faqs.answer: Patience — dormant bulbs routinely sit four to six weeks before waking, then leaf out quickly once they do. Floating a stubborn bulb at the surface for a few days often coaxes it into sprouting. Only give up on a bulb that has turned soft and foul-smelling; a firm one is still alive.

propagation: Cut the top halves of stems and replant them; the rooted bases branch into multiple new shoots.
careSummary: Rotala rotundifolia is the standard background stem of nature-style aquascaping: fast, forgiving, and quick to form the soft pink-orange bushes seen behind iwagumi stone work. It survives low-tech easily (staying green), and shifts pink to red as light intensity climbs.

faqs.question: How do I get my Rotala to turn red?
faqs.answer: Color follows light first: the tops only blush pink-to-red under strong light, with CO2 and lean nitrates deepening it further. In a low-tech tank it stays an attractive green — healthy, just not red.

faqs.question: How do I make Rotala bushy instead of stringy?
faqs.answer: Trim early and often. Each cut stem splits into two or more shoots, so two or three rounds of topping and replanting transform a sparse bunch into a dense bush. Crowded, untrimmed stems shed their lower leaves in their own shade.

propagation: Replant trimmed tops or even single leaves — fragments root with almost weedy enthusiasm. For a carpet or low bush, lay whole stems flat on the substrate and pin them down; vertical shoots rise from every node along the horizontal stem.
careSummary: Water Wisteria is a fast, lacy-leaved stem plant that thrives on neglect and drinks up excess nutrients, making it one of the best plants for a new tank fighting algae. Its dense, feathery growth doubles as cover for bettas, fry, and shrimp.

faqs.question: Why do my Water Wisteria leaves look different from when I bought them?
faqs.answer: The species changes leaf shape with conditions — narrow and finely divided in warm, bright tanks; broader and flatter when grown cooler, dimmer, or emersed. A change in leaf form is adaptation, not disease.

faqs.question: Can I float Water Wisteria instead of planting it?
faqs.answer: Yes. It grows happily as a floating thicket, where it gets more light and CO2 and grows even faster — a favorite trick for fry tanks and for outcompeting algae during a tank's first months.

propagation: Top the stems and replant the cuttings; bare lower stems regrow side shoots.
careSummary: Bacopa caroliniana is the slow-and-steady stem plant: thick succulent-like leaves on upright stalks that hold their shape between trims, ideal for orderly Dutch-style rows. It is among the most forgiving stems for low-tech tanks, asking only decent light. Under strong light the upper leaves blush copper to bronze-pink, and stems allowed to break the surface of an open-top tank can produce small blue-purple flowers.

faqs.question: Why does my Bacopa smell like lemon when trimmed?
faqs.answer: Crushed leaves of this species release a citrus-mint scent — it is sometimes grown as a pond herb for exactly that reason. The smell is normal and harmless to the tank.

faqs.question: Why is my Bacopa losing its lower leaves?
faqs.answer: Lower-leaf drop means the base is shaded, usually by the stem's own canopy. Trim and replant the healthy tops a little farther apart so light reaches down each stem, and the new growth will stay clothed to the substrate.

propagation: Replant trimmed tops; side shoots appear quickly on the cut bases.
careSummary: Ludwigia repens is the easiest red stem plant in the hobby — coppery-red undersides even in modest setups, deepening to full red as light increases. Fast, adaptable, and tolerant of everything from low-tech 10-gallons to high-energy Dutch tanks.

faqs.question: Why is my Ludwigia more green than red?
faqs.answer: Light intensity is the main driver: under low light the tops grow olive-green with red undersides, and stronger light brings the whole top half into red. Iron-rich fertilization and good nitrate levels help, but light does most of the work.

faqs.question: How fast does Ludwigia repens grow?
faqs.answer: In a healthy tank, expect an inch or more a week — it will reach the surface of a standard tank within a month or two. Plan on topping and replanting every few weeks, which conveniently thickens the bush each time.

propagation: Top and replant stems; cuttings root in one to two weeks.
careSummary: Alternanthera reineckii delivers true magenta-to-burgundy foliage that no light spectrum trick can fake — the centerpiece red of Dutch aquascaping. It holds its color even at moderate light, but repays CO2 and steady iron-rich fertilization with dramatically fuller growth.

faqs.question: Is Alternanthera reineckii possible in a low-tech tank?
faqs.answer: Yes, with the right expectations: growth is slow and the plant stays smaller, but the red color persists since it is pigment-driven rather than purely light-driven. Give it root tabs, decent light, and patience; avoid burying it in fast-growing neighbors that will outcompete it.

faqs.question: Why are my AR's lower leaves melting or curling?
faqs.answer: AR telegraphs nutrient instability — fluctuating CO2 or a lean water column shows up first as curled tips and deteriorating lower leaves. Consistent dosing (especially potassium and iron) and stable CO2 levels straighten out new growth within a few weeks.

propagation: Replant trimmed tops; thick stems can be cut into multiple sections and each will sprout.
careSummary: Hygrophila corymbosa is a big, broad-leaved background stem that turns a bare back wall into a green hedge in weeks, no CO2 required. Its size suits 20-gallon tanks and up, where its bold leaves contrast beautifully against fine-textured plants.

faqs.question: Why are the leaves on my Temple Plant melting after purchase?
faqs.answer: Most Temple Plants are farmed emersed, and the broad emersed leaves die back as submerged growth takes over — often dramatically. Keep the stems planted and lit; the new underwater leaves are longer, narrower, and permanent.

faqs.question: How do I keep Hygrophila corymbosa from overwhelming the tank?
faqs.answer: Hard pruning. Cut stems down by half whenever they crowd the surface and remove whole stems where the bush gets too wide — it regrows from every cut. Its appetite also makes it a useful nutrient sponge in tanks prone to algae.

propagation: Break a stem anywhere — every fragment keeps growing. There are no true roots; anchor it loosely or let it float.
careSummary: Hornwort is less a plant you grow than a green engine you deploy: rootless, indestructible across a 35-degree temperature range, and so fast-growing it routinely out-eats algae in new setups. Ideal for coldwater tanks, ponds, fry cover, and cycling tanks. It also releases allelopathic compounds that actively suppress algae, and it is one of the few plants goldfish do not shred within days.

faqs.question: Why is my Hornwort dropping needles everywhere?
faqs.answer: Needle drop is its trademark response to changed conditions — new water, new light, or being moved. The stems usually regrow cleanly once settled, but expect to clean the shed needles for a week or two after any change.

faqs.question: Can Hornwort be planted in substrate?
faqs.answer: It has no roots, so 'planting' means anchoring the stem ends under a rock or in a weight — buried ends often rot. Most keepers either float it or pin it loosely and let the upper growth do its thing.

propagation: Cut and replant stem sections; side shoots and roots form at the nodes.
careSummary: Anacharis is the classic coldwater workhorse — fast, cheap, and content from an unheated goldfish tank to a tropical community. Plant it in bunches or float it; either way it soaks up nutrients at a rate that makes life hard for algae. Check local regulations before pond use, as it is restricted in some states.

faqs.question: Why is my Anacharis falling apart at the bottom?
faqs.answer: The lower stem rots when it is shaded by its own dense top growth or crushed too deep into substrate. Plant stems individually and shallow, or float them; when a stem thins at the base, cut and replant the healthy top.

faqs.question: Is Anacharis good for goldfish tanks?
faqs.answer: It is one of the few plants that thrives in goldfish temperatures — and goldfish will graze it. In practice it grows fast enough that a decent bunch keeps ahead of the nibbling, doubling as a vegetable supplement.

propagation: Adventitious plantlets sprout along mature fronds; pluck them off once they have roots and float or plant them.
careSummary: Water Sprite is a fast, lacy fern that works planted or floating, growing dense thickets that bettas and fry treat as furniture. Like Water Wisteria it is a nutrient sponge for young tanks, but with a finer, softer texture.

faqs.question: Is Water Sprite better planted or floating?
faqs.answer: Both work; floating is faster. At the surface it gets stronger light and unlimited CO2, growing into a luxuriant raft with dangling roots fry love. Planted, it makes a soft green column — just keep the thick crown above the substrate so it does not rot.

faqs.question: Why is my Water Sprite disintegrating?
faqs.answer: Old fronds naturally die back as the plant pushes new ones, and a recently moved plant may shed most of its original growth. As long as new fronds keep coming from the crown, it is healthy; remove the dying fronds and thin the plantlets it leaves behind.

propagation: Daughter plants bud off on short runners; separate them or scoop out excess by the handful.
careSummary: Amazon Frogbit floats rosettes of round, lily-pad leaves with long feathery roots that shelter fry and shrimp while stripping nutrients from the water column. It dims the tank below — a feature for low-light plants and shy fish, a tradeoff above carpets.

faqs.question: Why is my Frogbit rotting from the top?
faqs.answer: Frogbit dies when its leaf tops stay wet — usually from filter splash, condensation drip, or being pushed under by strong flow. Corral it with airline tubing or a floating ring in the calmest corner and keep the lid from dripping on it.

faqs.question: Will Frogbit steal light from my other plants?
faqs.answer: Left unthinned, yes — a full canopy can cut light below it dramatically. Keep it to a fixed zone with a floating corral and remove a portion weekly; the shade it casts is also a legitimate tool for algae control and light-shy fish.

propagation: Divides on its own at the surface; separate clusters by hand and remove excess weekly once established.
careSummary: Red Root Floaters are the showpiece floating plant: under strong light the leaves flush deep red and the trailing crimson roots match. They need calmer water and more light than Frogbit, and reward iron dosing with the saturated color they are named for.

faqs.question: Why are my Red Root Floaters green instead of red?
faqs.answer: Color is light-driven: under modest light the leaves stay green with red roots only. High light at the surface — which floaters get more of than any other plant — plus iron-containing fertilizer brings the full red within a couple of weeks.

faqs.question: Why do my Red Root Floaters keep dying in my tank?
faqs.answer: Almost always surface agitation: they hate being splashed or swirled, and wet leaf tops rot. Calm a corner of the surface with a corral and reposition the filter outflow, and they go from melting to multiplying.

propagation: Forms daughter plants on short runners around the mother rosette; separate them once they carry several leaves.
careSummary: Crypt Lutea is a slightly taller, darker cousin of Crypt Wendtii with smooth olive-green leaves on upright stalks — equally bulletproof in low light without CO2. Plant it in nutrient-rich substrate or feed it root tabs, leave it alone through the initial melt, and it will quietly form a dense colony.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Crypt Lutea and Crypt Wendtii?
faqs.answer: Lutea holds its leaves more upright and stays a uniform olive green, while Wendtii grows lower and ruffled in green, bronze, or red forms. Care is identical, so the choice is purely aesthetic — Lutea reads as a tidy vertical accent, Wendtii as a spreading rosette.

faqs.question: Why did my Crypt Lutea lose all its leaves after planting?
faqs.answer: That is standard crypt melt: the plant sheds leaves grown in farm conditions and regrows ones adapted to your water. Do not pull it out — as long as the roots and crown stay firm, new leaves appear within two to four weeks.

propagation: Spreads by short runners; split off rooted daughter plants or let the clump expand into a low thicket.
careSummary: Crypt Lucens is one of the smallest cryptocorynes — narrow, glossy green leaves rarely topping four inches — making it the easiest path to a planted foreground without carpet-plant demands. It needs only low light, root feeding, and patience while it slowly knits into a grassy stand.

faqs.question: Can Crypt Lucens replace a carpet plant in a low-tech tank?
faqs.answer: Effectively, yes. It will never form a flat lawn like Monte Carlo, but planted in a tight grid it grows into a low, grassy foreground band that needs no CO2, no strong light, and almost no trimming — just expect months rather than weeks for full coverage.

faqs.question: How fast does Crypt Lucens spread?
faqs.answer: Slowly — a healthy plant sends out a few runners per month once established. Buying several pots and splitting each into individual plantlets spaced an inch or two apart is the practical way to shortcut the wait.

propagation: Top the stems and replant the cuttings; the cut bases branch into multiple new shoots.
careSummary: Willow Hygro grows long, narrow leaves that arch and sway in the current like a submerged willow — the easiest way to add motion to a tall background. It is a typical Hygrophila: fast, hungry, and happy without CO2, so plan on regular topping once it hits the surface.

faqs.question: Why are my Willow Hygro leaves narrow on new growth but broad on old growth?
faqs.answer: The broad leaves were grown emersed at the farm and will gradually die back; the long, ribbon-like leaves are its true submerged form. Trim away the old foliage as it fades and replant healthy tops.

faqs.question: How much flow does Hygrophila angustifolia need?
faqs.answer: It does not require strong current, but it looks best with some — the long leaves are made to trail in moving water. Position it across from the filter outflow and the whole stand ripples, which is most of this plant's charm.

propagation: Replant trimmed tops; even leaf-bearing side shoots root readily when pushed into substrate.
careSummary: Siamensis is the mid-sized member of the corymbosa family — smaller leaves and a bushier habit than the full Temple Plant, with the same iron constitution. It thrives in low light without CO2 and grows fast enough to serve as a nutrient sponge in newly set-up tanks.

faqs.question: Is Hygrophila siamensis good for a beginner tank?
faqs.answer: It is one of the best: cheap, fast, tolerant of hard water and low light, and forgiving of erratic fertilization. Its appetite for nitrogen also helps suppress algae during a tank's unstable first months.

faqs.question: How do I keep Siamensis bushy instead of tall and bare?
faqs.answer: Top the stems every few weeks before they reach the surface and replant the cuttings in front of the originals. Each cut stem throws two or more side shoots, so a sparse row becomes a dense hedge after two or three trim cycles.

propagation: Top and replant stems; cuttings root within a week or two and the bases regrow side shoots.
careSummary: Ludwigia arcuata trades the broad leaves of its relatives for fine needles that turn coppery orange-red under strong light, giving a delicate texture few red plants offer. A North American native, it tolerates cooler water than most reds, but it needs genuinely high light to color and stay compact.

faqs.question: Why is my Ludwigia arcuata green and leggy instead of red and bushy?
faqs.answer: Light intensity is almost the whole story with this species. Under moderate light it grows green, sparse, and stretched; under high light at the substrate it tightens up and shifts orange-red. CO2 and iron help, but they cannot substitute for light.

faqs.question: Is Ludwigia arcuata the same as Rotala for aquascaping purposes?
faqs.answer: They fill a similar fine-textured midground role, but arcuata branches less aggressively and holds a needle-like leaf that reads spikier than Rotala's soft ovals. Many scapers mix the two — Rotala for volume, arcuata for accents of contrasting texture.

propagation: Replant trimmed tops; stems branch from every node left behind.
careSummary: Ludwigia palustris is a compact, small-leaved red stem that colors up at lower light than almost any other red plant — solid medium light produces rusty red tops even without CO2. Its modest height makes it one of the few reds that fits comfortably in the midground of a small tank.

faqs.question: How is Ludwigia palustris different from Ludwigia repens?
faqs.answer: Palustris is smaller in every dimension — shorter stems, smaller leaves — and generally reddens more readily at moderate light. Repens grows taller and faster, making it the better pure background plant, while palustris suits midground bushes and nano tanks.

faqs.question: Can Ludwigia palustris turn red without CO2 injection?
faqs.answer: Yes — it is one of the most reliable low-tech reds. Give it unshaded medium-to-strong light and keep nitrates on the leaner side, and the upper halves of the stems develop a genuine red rather than the olive blush most species manage without gas.

propagation: Top and replant; frequent trimming multiplies side shoots and keeps the bush dense and low.
careSummary: Super Red Mini is a selected form of Ludwigia palustris that stays small and turns a deep blood-red with far less effort than classic high-tech reds. It is arguably the easiest truly red plant in the hobby — medium light gets you most of the color, and CO2 simply makes it faster and fuller. Dosing an iron-rich fertilizer deepens the red noticeably beyond what light alone achieves.

faqs.question: Does Ludwigia Super Red Mini really stay red in a low-tech tank?
faqs.answer: Mostly, yes. The cultivar was selected for pigment, so even at medium light the new growth comes in red rather than green. Without CO2 it grows slower and the lower leaves may dull, but the tops keep their color — something few red plants manage.

faqs.question: How do I keep Super Red Mini compact?
faqs.answer: Trim early and often. Cutting stems back by a third every couple of weeks forces branching at the base, building a tight red mound a few inches tall instead of sparse vertical stems. Replant the trimmings to widen the patch.

propagation: Top the stems and replant; healthy stands need topping weekly, and every cutting roots.
careSummary: Ludwigia 'Cuba' is a showpiece for high-tech tanks: whorls of narrow copper-orange leaves that can dominate a Dutch background — and a plant that genuinely will not perform without injected CO2, high light, and rich, consistent fertilization. In a properly run tank it grows explosively; in anything less it stunts and stalls.

faqs.question: Why are the growing tips of my Ludwigia Cuba twisted and stunted?
faqs.answer: Stunted tips on this species almost always trace to instability — fluctuating CO2 is the usual offender, followed by micronutrient gaps (iron and the other traces). Verify your CO2 holds steady through the photoperiod and dose a complete micro fertilizer daily; new tips should grow out clean within a week or two.

faqs.question: Can I grow Ludwigia Cuba without CO2?
faqs.answer: Realistically, no. It may survive for a while, but it will shrink, lose its whorled form, and eventually waste away. This is one of the species where injected CO2 is a requirement, not an upgrade — without it, choose Ludwigia palustris or Super Red Mini instead.

propagation: Top sturdy stems and replant the cuttings; the thick stems root slowly but reliably.
careSummary: Ammannia gracilis is a big, architectural stem from West Africa with long, wavy leaves in copper-bronze to salmon-orange — a Dutch-tank classic that earns its place under high light and generous iron dosing. It is bulkier and slower than Rotala-type stems, so give each stem room to develop rather than packing it into a tight bunch.

faqs.question: Why is the new growth on my Ammannia pale or whitish?
faqs.answer: Pale, washed-out tips on Ammannia are the classic sign of iron deficiency — this genus is one of the hungriest iron feeders in the hobby. Dose a chelated iron or comprehensive micro mix several times a week and new leaves should come in with proper bronze-orange color.

faqs.question: Does Ammannia gracilis need CO2?
faqs.answer: It will survive without injection in soft, well-lit water, but it grows leggy and dull. With CO2 it transforms — larger leaves, saturated color, and a sturdy upright habit. Treat CO2 as strongly recommended rather than strictly required.

propagation: Top and replant stems before they reach the surface; tips left to crowd the light tend to stunt.
careSummary: Pogostemon stellatus crowns each stem with a starburst of narrow leaves that flush pink-to-purple under intense light — one of the most striking background stems in aquascaping, and one of the fussiest. It demands injected CO2, high light, soft water, and uninterrupted fertilization; any lapse shows up within days as stunted, curled tips.

faqs.question: Why does my Pogostemon stellatus keep stunting at the tips?
faqs.answer: This species is an early-warning system for instability: inconsistent CO2, a missed week of micros, or a swing in water hardness all stop the growing tip. Restore stable conditions, cut the stem below the stunted crown, and let a fresh side shoot take over — the damaged tip itself rarely recovers.

faqs.question: Is the narrow-leaf form of Pogostemon stellatus easier than the broad form?
faqs.answer: Somewhat. The narrow-leaf type tolerates slightly leaner conditions and stays more compact, which is why it is the form most stores carry. It still belongs firmly in high-tech territory — CO2 and strong light are non-negotiable for healthy growth.

propagation: Top the stems and replant; repeated trimming builds the dense, mounded bushes the cultivar is known for.
careSummary: Rotala 'H'ra' is the color-upgraded form of Rotala rotundifolia, shifting orange-red under high light where the standard species only blushes pink. It keeps the family's forgiving nature and fast growth, so it is the sensible first red-bush plant for anyone moving from low-tech toward a lit, fertilized aquascape.

faqs.question: How do I get the deepest red out of Rotala H'ra?
faqs.answer: Strong light is the foundation; from there, keeping nitrates lean (roughly 5–10 ppm) while maintaining phosphate, potassium, and iron pushes the tops from orange toward true red. In nutrient-rich or dimly lit tanks it grows fine but stays orange-green.

faqs.question: Is Rotala H'ra harder to grow than regular Rotala rotundifolia?
faqs.answer: No — the care is essentially identical and it survives low-tech tanks the same way, just without the color payoff. The only real difference is that H'ra needs the high-light, lean-nitrate treatment to show why you bought it.

propagation: Top and replant stems; cuttings root in one to two weeks and the variegation carries over true.
careSummary: Rosanervig is the variegated form of Alternanthera reineckii: the same pink-to-magenta leaves, but threaded with pale, almost white veins that read like neon under good light. Care matches standard AR — moderate light minimum, root tabs plus water-column iron, and patience with its unhurried growth.

faqs.question: Are the white veins on Rosanervig a deficiency?
faqs.answer: No — the light veination is the stable trait this cultivar was selected for, and it actually intensifies when the plant is thriving. Deficiency on AR looks different: curled tips, melting lower leaves, or overall pale washed-out color rather than crisp bright veins.

faqs.question: Where should I place Rosanervig in the aquascape?
faqs.answer: Midground, with clear line of sight and nothing overshadowing it. It grows too slowly to compete with background stems, and its pink-and-white pattern is the kind of focal accent that works best as a deliberate cluster of three to five stems rather than scattered singles.

propagation: Cut and replant rooted sections or trimmed tops; trimming triggers side shoots from the nodes.
careSummary: Juncus repens is a true aquatic rush — stiff, grassy blades that creep and angle through the midground, adding a wild meadow texture no stem or carpet plant replicates. A hardy North American native, it handles a wide range of temperatures and chemistry and grows slowly enough to stay where you put it.

faqs.question: Why is my Juncus repens turning reddish at the tips?
faqs.answer: Under high light the new growth takes on a coppery-red tint — a normal and generally desirable response, not a deficiency. In moderate light the plant stays grass green.

faqs.question: Does Juncus repens stay upright or creep along the substrate?
faqs.answer: Both, depending on light. Under strong light it hugs lower and creeps, rooting from nodes as it goes; in dimmer tanks it grows more vertically toward the surface. Trimming the tallest shoots encourages the low, spreading habit most aquascapers want.

propagation: Replant trimmed tops; stems root quickly from the lower nodes.
careSummary: Golden Lloydiella carries round, butter-yellow leaves up sturdy stems — an instant color contrast against standard green plants without any of the demands of red species. It is a temperate plant at heart: it shines in unheated and cool-water tanks and grows steadily but slowly when fully submerged.

faqs.question: Is Golden Lloydiella good for coldwater or goldfish tanks?
faqs.answer: Yes — it is one of the best. It genuinely prefers temperatures in the 60s and low 70s, and its leaves are firm enough that goldfish usually leave it alone. In tanks kept above the high 70s it tends to thin out and decline over time.

faqs.question: Why does my Golden Lloydiella grow so slowly?
faqs.answer: Slow, steady growth is normal for this species underwater — it is a marginal pond plant that merely tolerates full submersion. Cooler water, decent light, and clean conditions keep it healthy; do not expect stem-plant speed, and treat its pace as a low-maintenance feature.

propagation: Plantlets form on submerged flower stalks; cut them free once rooted, or divide a large crown.
careSummary: The Marble Queen is a variegated radican sword, splashing cream and white across broad, spade-shaped green leaves — a centerpiece plant that needs no CO2, just deep substrate and steady root feeding. Being an Echinodorus cordifolius, it will try to grow emergent leaves out of the water in shallow tanks, so plan on pruning the climbers.

faqs.question: Why does my Marble Queen keep sending leaves above the water?
faqs.answer: Emergent growth is this species' natural habit — in the wild it stands half out of the water. Cutting aerial leaves and their stalks at the base redirects energy into submerged foliage; deeper tanks and slightly less intense light also reduce the urge to climb.

faqs.question: Will the white variegation stay, or fade over time?
faqs.answer: The marbling is a stable cultivar trait, but it is strongest on new leaves grown in good conditions. Weak, starved plants produce smaller, plainer leaves — keep root tabs under the crown every couple of months and the fresh growth will carry bold cream patterns.

propagation: Spreads by runners across the substrate; snip off daughter plants once they have a few leaves, or let the colony fill in.
careSummary: Vallisneria spiralis is the tank-sized Val: the same effortless ribbon-grass look as Jungle Val, but topping out around two feet instead of three, so it suits standard aquariums without constant leaf-folding at the surface. Low light, no CO2, and hard alkaline water are all fine — plant it, leave the crown exposed, and let the runners work.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Vallisneria spiralis and Jungle Val?
faqs.answer: Mostly size. Spiralis has narrower leaves that stop around 20–24 inches, while Jungle Val regularly hits a yard. For tanks under roughly 75 gallons, spiralis gives the flowing Val curtain without the surface mat of excess leaf. The 'spiralis' name refers to its corkscrewed flower stalk, not the leaves.

faqs.question: Why is my Vallisneria not spreading?
faqs.answer: Vals sulk for a few weeks after planting, especially if the crown was buried — keep the white crown just above the substrate. Once rooted, runners come steadily; if they still do not, the usual culprits are very soft, acidic water (Vals prefer some hardness) or a recent dose of glutaraldehyde-based liquid carbon, which this genus tolerates poorly.

propagation: Runs aggressively via chains of plantlets; snip and replant daughters to direct the spread, or let it colonize.
careSummary: The narrow-leaf chain sword is the fastest natural lawn for a medium-light tank: grass-like rosettes that fire off runner after runner until the foreground is a meadow. It is more forgiving than true carpet plants — no CO2 required — though it rewards a nutrient-rich substrate and turns bronze under strong light.

faqs.question: How quickly will chain swords carpet my foreground?
faqs.answer: In a fertile substrate with solid medium light, a handful of starter plants can cover a couple of square feet in two to three months — runners appear within weeks of planting. Without root nutrition the spread slows dramatically, so root tabs are the cheapest accelerator.

faqs.question: Why is my chain sword growing taller than expected?
faqs.answer: In lower light the leaves stretch to five inches or more, reaching for energy. Stronger light keeps rosettes squat at two to three inches and brings out a reddish-bronze tint. If it has already stretched, improve the light and trim — new growth comes in shorter.

propagation: Trim and replant tops, or push pinches of cuttings into the substrate; every fragment with a node grows.
careSummary: Pearl Weed is the shape-shifter of aquascaping: trimmed hard it creeps into a tight carpet, left taller it builds dense bright-green mounds and hedges, all on tiny paired leaves. It is far less demanding than the look suggests — medium light and regular trimming do most of the work, with CO2 as an accelerant rather than a requirement.

faqs.question: Can Pearl Weed form a carpet without CO2?
faqs.answer: Yes — it is one of the few plants that can. Under medium-high light, plant small clumps an inch apart and trim flat every week or two; the repeated cutting forces horizontal, creeping growth. Without CO2 it carpets thicker and taller than Monte Carlo, more like a dense half-inch to one-inch turf.

faqs.question: Why does my Pearl Weed carpet keep lifting off the substrate?
faqs.answer: Pearl Weed roots lightly, and once the mat thickens, the shaded bottom layer dies and the whole sheet peels up. Trim aggressively to keep the mat under an inch and replant any lifted sections as fresh cuttings — thin and anchored beats thick and floating.

propagation: Divide a mat into small plugs and plant them in a grid; runners knit the plugs into a continuous carpet.
careSummary: Dwarf Baby Tears is the iconic iwagumi carpet — the smallest-leaved aquarium plant in the trade, forming a dense lawn of millimeter foliage that pearls with oxygen under bright light. It is also honestly demanding: high light, injected CO2, and fine, fertile substrate are effectively prerequisites, and shortcuts usually end in a yellowing, lifting mat. It is a surprisingly recent discovery, found in Cuba by Danish botanist Holger Windeløv in 2003.

faqs.question: Can I grow Dwarf Baby Tears without CO2?
faqs.answer: Long-term, almost never — submerged HC without CO2 thins, yellows, and dissolves over a few months. The one workaround is the dry-start method: grow the carpet emersed on damp aquasoil for six to eight weeks (it gets unlimited CO2 from air), then flood. Even then, the established carpet holds far better with injection after flooding.

faqs.question: Why did my HC carpet detach and float up in one piece?
faqs.answer: HC roots are shallow, and a carpet left untrimmed traps gas and dead growth underneath until buoyancy wins. Trim it down by a third regularly so light reaches the base, avoid uprooting fish like large cories in the carpet zone, and press any lifting edges back in immediately.

propagation: Divide an established pad and tie or glue the fragments to new hardscape; any healthy frond cluster regrows.
careSummary: Fissidens nobilis grows in flat, feathery fronds that look less like typical aquarium moss and more like miniature ferns carpeting a stone — among the most refined textures available for hardscape. It is undemanding in low light without CO2, just genuinely slow, and unlike stringy mosses it holds its tidy shape with almost no trimming. Its fronds brown in warm water, so keep the tank under about 78°F for the healthiest growth.

faqs.question: How long does Fissidens take to attach and fill in?
faqs.answer: Longer than Java or Christmas Moss — plan on one to two months for its rhizoids to grip the hardscape and several more for a lush pad. Tie it firmly with thread or use gel superglue on dry stone, keep some gentle flow over it, and resist rearranging while it anchors.

faqs.question: Why is my Fissidens collecting algae?
faqs.answer: Slow growth plus fine fronds make it an algae magnet under too much light. Keep it shaded or under genuinely low light, maintain good flow so debris does not settle, and let amano shrimp graze it — they clean the fronds without damaging them.

propagation: Daughter rosettes bud off on short stolons; separate them by hand or scoop out excess weekly.
careSummary: Water Lettuce floats velvety, ribbed rosettes with long feathery roots that fish fry and shrimp treat as nursery habitat, while the plant itself strips nitrates faster than almost anything else in the tank. Indoors it stays palm-sized rather than pond-sized; check your state and local regulations before buying, as it is a restricted invasive species in several warm-climate states.

faqs.question: Why does my Water Lettuce stay small or turn yellow in the aquarium?
faqs.answer: Indoors it naturally grows much smaller than the dinner-plate rosettes seen in ponds, so small is normal. Yellowing usually means nutrient hunger — floaters feed entirely from the water column, so dose a liquid fertilizer — or leaf rot from condensation dripping off a tight lid onto the velvety leaves, which must stay dry.

faqs.question: Is Water Lettuce legal to keep?
faqs.answer: Not everywhere. Because it chokes waterways in warm climates, several US states (Florida and Texas among them) restrict or prohibit it. Check your state's invasive species list before ordering, and never dispose of trimmings in drains, ponds, or waterways — bag them for the trash.

propagation: Chains of paired leaves divide on their own; lift out handfuls to thin, and any fragment keeps growing.
careSummary: Salvinia minima is a small floating fern whose fuzzy, water-repellent leaf pairs spread into a quilted surface mat — large enough to net out easily, unlike duckweed, but just as effective at shading and nitrate export. It is a restricted species in some states (the related giant salvinia is a federal noxious weed), so verify local regulations and never release trimmings into waterways.

faqs.question: Is Salvinia better than duckweed or frogbit for a small tank?
faqs.answer: For most people, yes. It controls nutrients and shade like duckweed but comes out by the handful when you want it gone, and it tolerates surface agitation and lid condensation better than frogbit. Its roots are shorter than frogbit's, which some prefer visually.

faqs.question: Why are my Salvinia leaves browning or sinking?
faqs.answer: The usual causes are nutrient-starved water (floaters depend entirely on liquid fertilizer in a lightly stocked tank), water constantly splashing onto the leaf tops, or overcrowding so severe the mat shades itself. Thin the mat weekly and calm the surface where it floats.

propagation: Each tiny frond buds off daughters continuously — a colony can double in under a week; thin by scooping.
careSummary: Duckweed is the fastest nutrient exporter in the hobby: a film of pinhead-sized fronds that doubles in days, starving algae and feeding herbivorous fish in the process. Be sure you want it before adding it — eradicating it later is notoriously difficult — and check local regulations before pond use or disposal, as moving it into natural waterways is prohibited in many areas.

faqs.question: How do I get rid of duckweed once it takes over?
faqs.answer: Persistently. Scoop the surface daily with a fine net, run a surface skimmer, and dim the light for a couple of weeks to slow regrowth — every missed frond restarts the colony. Many keepers simply manage it instead, removing 80 percent weekly and treating the harvest as free nitrate removal.

faqs.question: Is duckweed actually good for anything in an aquarium?
faqs.answer: Genuinely, yes. It outcompetes algae for nitrogen, shades skittish fish, and is nutritious live food for goldfish and other herbivores. In heavily stocked or hard-to-plant tanks it is one of the cheapest water-quality tools available — the only catch is that it never leaves.

faqs.question: How do I keep duckweed from spreading to my other tanks?
faqs.answer: Treat it like a contagion: single fronds hitchhike on nets, plant bunches, shipping bags, and wet hands, and one frond is enough to seed a new colony. Dedicate equipment to the duckweed tank or let nets dry fully between uses, inspect incoming plants closely, and rinse anything shared before it touches another aquarium.

propagation: Top the stems and replant; the thick stems root slowly but dependably, and bases resprout.
careSummary: The dwarf cardinal plant grows rounded, almost succulent bright-green leaves on stout upright stems — the classic plant for the Dutch 'street', a diagonal walkway of identical plants through the foreground. It is slow, tidy, and undemanding at medium light, holding its shape between trims better than nearly any stem plant.

faqs.question: Why is it called the cardinal plant when it is green?
faqs.answer: The name comes from its emersed life: grown above water in ponds and bogs, Lobelia cardinalis throws brilliant scarlet flower spikes. Underwater it stays compact and green — purple-tinged stems at most — and never flowers, so aquarists are buying it for form, not color.

faqs.question: How do I create the Dutch street look with Lobelia cardinalis?
faqs.answer: Plant a single-file line of stems diagonally from the front glass toward the midground, spaced about an inch apart, and keep them stair-stepped in height by trimming the front stems shorter than the rear. Its uniform leaf shape and slow growth are what make the effect maintainable.

propagation: Mature bulbs can be carefully divided if they form multiple crowns; flower spikes occasionally set seed at the surface.
careSummary: The Madagascar Lace Plant grows leaves that are pure latticework — a skeleton of veins with open windows between them, unlike anything else in the hobby. It is a genuine specialist's plant: it wants cooler water than most tropicals, pristine well-oxygenated conditions, rich root feeding, and tolerance for its habit of resting between growth flushes.

faqs.question: Why did my lace plant suddenly stop growing and lose its leaves?
faqs.answer: Aponogetons grow from a bulb and many follow a natural rhythm of lush growth followed by a rest phase where leaves die back. Do not discard the bulb — leave it planted (or rest it in cooler water for a few weeks) and it typically resprouts. Steady cool temperatures and fresh root tabs encourage the next flush.

faqs.question: How do I keep algae out of the lattice leaves?
faqs.answer: The open mesh traps debris and grows algae faster than solid leaves, and an algae-coated lace leaf rarely recovers. Keep light moderate rather than intense, maintain good flow and frequent water changes, and employ a cleanup crew — amano shrimp and small snails graze the lattice without harming it.

faqs.question: Is the Madagascar Lace Plant suitable for a beginner?
faqs.answer: It is a poor first plant but a reasonable first challenge plant. The bulb gives it reserves that forgive early mistakes, and its actual demands — cooler water, clean conditions, root fertilization — are about consistency rather than equipment. Treat the price of the bulb as tuition.

propagation: Detach the plantlets that form on the finely branched leaf tips, or divide the rhizome into sections and reattach each to hardscape.
careSummary: 'Windelov' is a Java Fern selection whose leaf tips split into delicate, antler-like crests, giving it a softer, lacier silhouette than the plain species. Care is identical and just as forgiving — low light, no CO2, rhizome tied or glued to wood or stone and never buried — so it is an easy way to add fine texture to a low-tech tank. The cultivar takes its name from Holger Windeløv, the Danish plantsman who introduced it to the hobby.

faqs.question: How is 'Windelov' different from regular Java Fern?
faqs.answer: Only in leaf shape. Where standard Java Fern ends in a simple point, 'Windelov' fans out into a frilly, finely divided crest, and it tends to stay a bit smaller and bushier. Growing requirements, hardiness, and the rule about keeping the rhizome exposed are all the same.

faqs.question: Why are the tips of my Windelov turning brown?
faqs.answer: The intricate tips are the first place to show stress from strong light or poor water flow, and a little browning at the very ends is common as the plant settles in. Trim the worst-affected leaves, keep it in a shadier spot, and let new growth come in; persistent black patches usually mean the rhizome got buried.

propagation: Pull the plantlets that sprout along older leaves and reattach them, or split the rhizome into pieces with several leaves each.
careSummary: Narrow-Leaf Java Fern carries slim, upright blades a fraction the width of the standard plant, reading as finer and more orderly while keeping every bit of the species' toughness. It thrives in low light without CO2 and attaches to wood or rock by its rhizome — just keep that rhizome above the substrate so it does not rot.

faqs.question: Does Narrow-Leaf Java Fern grow faster than the regular kind?
faqs.answer: Not really faster, but it tends to send up more individual leaves, so a single plant fills out into a denser clump over time. It is still a slow grower overall, which is part of why it stays algae-prone on its oldest leaves.

faqs.question: Can I grow Narrow-Leaf Java Fern in a tall background spot?
faqs.answer: Yes — its upright, slender leaves reach 8 to 10 inches and look great as a midground-to-background plant on taller pieces of driftwood. Attach the rhizome partway up the wood and let the leaves rise; it does not need to be near the light to do well.

propagation: Cut the rhizome into sections with at least three or four leaves each and reattach the divisions to wood or stone.
careSummary: Anubias Barteri is the full-size cousin of Anubias Nana, with broad, leathery leaves on longer stalks that make it a genuine centerpiece epiphyte rather than a foreground accent. It shares the same bulletproof care — low light, no CO2, rhizome attached above the substrate — but commands far more visual space, so give it room on a large piece of driftwood.

faqs.question: How big does Anubias Barteri get compared to Anubias Nana?
faqs.answer: Considerably bigger. Where Nana tops out around 6 inches, full Barteri can reach 12 to 16 inches tall with leaves several inches across. Plan for it as a midground or even background feature in a standard tank rather than a small foreground plant.

faqs.question: Why is my Anubias Barteri growing so slowly?
faqs.answer: That is normal — all Anubias are slow by nature, often adding only a leaf or two a month. The trade-off is that they need almost no care and last for years. Gentle root and water-column feeding plus some CO2 will speed it up modestly, but patience is the real requirement.

propagation: Divide the rhizome into small sections, each with a few of its tiny leaves, and glue or tie them to nano hardscape.
careSummary: Anubias Nana 'Petite' is a miniature selection of Nana with leaves barely the size of a fingernail, making it the go-to epiphyte for nano tanks, bonsai trees, and detailed foreground rockwork. Care is the same as any Anubias — low light, no CO2, rhizome kept above the substrate — only at a much smaller scale. The cultivar originated at Oriental Aquarium in Singapore.

faqs.question: Is Anubias Petite good for a nano or shrimp tank?
faqs.answer: It is ideal. Its tiny leaves keep the scale right in small tanks, the dense low rhizome gives shrimp grazing surface, and it asks for no CO2 or strong light. Glue a few sections to small stones or branches and it will slowly knit into a compact cushion.

faqs.question: Why does my Anubias Petite get algae more than larger plants?
faqs.answer: Its leaves are small and extremely slow-growing, so each one stays in place a long time and collects algae, especially under bright light. Keep it shaded, run a modest photoperiod, and let amano shrimp or nerite snails keep the little leaves clean.

faqs.question: Why is part of my Petite's rhizome turning black and soft?
faqs.answer: That is rhizome rot, and it spreads if left alone. Trim away every mushy section with sterile scissors until only firm tissue remains, throw the trimmings out, and remount the healthy pieces somewhere with better flow so water keeps moving across the rhizome.

propagation: Cut the rhizome into sections with several leaves each and reattach the pieces to hardscape.
careSummary: Anubias Congensis grows long, narrow, slightly arched leaves on upright stalks, giving it a more elegant, vertical look than the rounder Barteri. It carries the usual Anubias toughness — low light, no CO2, rhizome above the substrate — and its height makes it a useful midground-to-background epiphyte that breaks up broad-leaved neighbors.

faqs.question: Where should I place Anubias Congensis in the tank?
faqs.answer: Its taller, upright form suits the midground or background, ideally attached partway up a piece of driftwood so the narrow leaves rise and arch. It contrasts nicely with rounder plants like Anubias Nana or broad swords.

faqs.question: Does Anubias Congensis need to be attached to wood or rock?
faqs.answer: It does best as an epiphyte with the rhizome tied or glued to hardscape and the roots free to wander. You can wedge the roots into substrate, but never bury the rhizome itself — buried rhizomes rot, which is the most common way to kill any Anubias.

propagation: Mature plants send up adventitious plantlets on flower stalks; once a plantlet has roots and several leaves, cut it free and plant it.
careSummary: Fancy Twist is a hybrid sword grown for its dramatically curled and rippled leaves, with new growth flushing pink or red over a green, lightly speckled base. It is a heavy root feeder like all swords — bury it deep with root tabs — and while it tolerates low-tech conditions, brighter light and steady feeding bring out the strongest leaf color and twist.

faqs.question: How do I get the best color and curl from Fancy Twist?
faqs.answer: Stronger light and consistent nutrients are the levers. Under medium-high light with regular root tabs, the new leaves come in pinker and more tightly rippled; in dimmer tanks it stays greener and flatter, though still healthy. CO2 is optional but thickens growth.

faqs.question: Why are the outer leaves of my Fancy Twist melting after I planted it?
faqs.answer: Most swords are grown emersed at the farm, and their original leaves often die back when first submerged while new aquatic leaves replace them. Trim off the mush, keep the crown above the substrate, and feed the roots; the replacement growth is permanent.

propagation: Produces plantlets on long flower stalks; detach each rooted plantlet once it has several leaves and plant it.
careSummary: The Green Ozelot Sword is a hardy hybrid sword with green leaves scattered with dark maroon-brown spots, giving a centerpiece plant a patterned, almost leopard-print look without demanding equipment. Like other swords it is a heavy root feeder that wants deep substrate and root tabs, and it reaches a substantial size suited to tanks of 20 gallons and up.

faqs.question: Will the spots on my Green Ozelot Sword fade?
faqs.answer: The dark speckling is most pronounced on new leaves and under decent light; in very dim tanks the contrast softens but rarely vanishes. Good root feeding keeps fresh, well-marked leaves coming, which is what maintains the spotted display.

faqs.question: Is the Green Ozelot Sword too big for a community tank?
faqs.answer: Only for small ones. In a 20-gallon-plus tank it makes an excellent background centerpiece, and angelfish and other midsize community fish appreciate the broad leaves. In tanks under about 15 gallons it will quickly dominate, so choose a smaller sword or crypt instead.

propagation: Sends up flower stalks bearing plantlets; once each has roots and a few leaves, separate it and plant it.
careSummary: The Red Flame Sword is a compact hybrid sword whose leaves are heavily mottled with deep red and burgundy over green, putting sword-plant color at a midground scale. It is a hungry root feeder that rewards root tabs and brighter light with more intense red; in low light the color mutes toward green-and-brown, so give it good light to earn the name.

faqs.question: How do I keep my Red Flame Sword red instead of green?
faqs.answer: Light drives the color. Under solid medium-to-high light with consistent root feeding the new leaves come in strongly red-mottled; in dim tanks they trend greener with only faint markings. CO2 is not required but produces fuller, more vividly colored growth.

faqs.question: Does the Red Flame Sword stay smaller than an Amazon Sword?
faqs.answer: Yes — it is a more compact hybrid, typically topping out around a foot rather than the 18-to-20 inches a full Amazon Sword reaches. That makes it a more manageable colorful centerpiece for medium tanks, though it still needs root-zone feeding to thrive.

propagation: Spreads by runners that pop up daughter plants nearby; snip the runners to relocate plantlets or let it form a low thicket.
careSummary: The Broad-Leaf Chain Sword is a small, grassy sword that spreads by runners into a low foreground-to-midground meadow, with leaves wider and a touch taller than its narrow-leaf relative. It is undemanding — no CO2 needed — but as a root feeder it does much better in nutrient-rich substrate or with root tabs, where it carpets far more quickly.

faqs.question: How is this different from the Narrow-Leaf Chain Sword?
faqs.answer: Mostly leaf width and height. The broad-leaf form has wider, slightly taller blades that read as a denser, leafier carpet, while the narrow-leaf version is finer and more grass-like. Both spread by runners and have nearly identical care.

faqs.question: Why won't my chain sword spread into a carpet?
faqs.answer: Chain swords feed mainly through their roots, so in lean gravel or sand they stall. Add root tabs around the plants and make sure light reaches the substrate; with food at the roots and enough light, they send out runners and fill in steadily.

propagation: Top the stems and replant the cuttings; side shoots branch from the trimmed bases.
careSummary: Limnophila aromatica is a showpiece stem plant whose narrow leaves carry green tops over rich purple-to-magenta undersides when grown well. Reaching that color is the catch: it wants high light, CO2, and steady iron-rich fertilization, and in low-tech tanks it survives but stays green and leggy. Plan on it as a centerpiece for an established high-energy setup.

faqs.question: Why is my Limnophila aromatica all green instead of purple?
faqs.answer: The purple develops only under strong light, and usually with CO2 and good iron and micronutrient levels. Under modest light the plant grows healthy but plain green. Raise the light intensity first; that is the single biggest factor in coloring it up.

faqs.question: Is Limnophila aromatica beginner-friendly?
faqs.answer: It will grow for a beginner, but it won't look like the catalog photos without high light, CO2, and consistent dosing. If you are running a low-tech tank, treat the green growth as the realistic outcome, or hold off until you have a CO2-injected setup.

propagation: Cut the tops and replant them; the rooted bases send out multiple new shoots.
careSummary: Limnophila hippuridoides grows whorls of feathery leaves that flush deep purple-red underneath under good light, giving a soft, bushy texture in the midground or background. It is more forgiving than its cousin L. aromatica — faster-growing and willing to take modest color even at medium light — though brighter light and CO2 deepen the reds considerably.

faqs.question: How do I make Limnophila hippuridoides bushy?
faqs.answer: Trim it regularly. Each cut stem branches into two or more shoots, so a couple of rounds of topping and replanting turn a thin bunch into a full bush. Left untrimmed it grows tall and shades out its own lower leaves.

faqs.question: Does this plant need CO2 to show color?
faqs.answer: Not strictly. Under medium light it takes on purple undersides without injection, just less intensely. CO2 plus stronger light pushes it toward a much richer red-violet and keeps the growth dense rather than stretched.

propagation: Replant trimmed tops; side shoots sprout readily from the cut bases.
careSummary: Ludwigia peruensis is a fast, large-leaved red stem plant that brings bold orange-to-red foliage to the background without the fussiness of finer Ludwigias. It colors up under medium-to-strong light, and like most red stems it deepens with iron-rich dosing and CO2 while staying greener and more olive in low-tech conditions.

faqs.question: How tall does Ludwigia peruensis get?
faqs.answer: It readily reaches the top of a standard tank, often 18 inches or more, with large leaves that make it a bold background plant. Top and replant it every few weeks to keep it bushy and within bounds; the cuttings root easily and thicken the stand.

faqs.question: Why are the new leaves bigger and redder than the old ones?
faqs.answer: Submerged new growth is where the color and size show best, while older leaves — especially any grown emersed at the farm — are smaller and may yellow off. As long as fresh red tops keep coming, the plant is healthy; remove tired lower leaves and replant strong tops.

propagation: Top the stems and replant the cuttings once they have firmed up; trimmed bases resprout.
careSummary: Ammannia senegalensis is a demanding Dutch-style stem plant whose leaves curl and glow coppery orange to red under intense light, the tops sometimes blushing pink. It genuinely needs CO2, high light, and a well-dosed water column to look its best — without them it stalls, pales, and drops leaves — so it is a plant for an established high-tech tank rather than a first attempt.

faqs.question: Why are the leaves on my Ammannia senegalensis curling?
faqs.answer: A gentle inward curl toward the light is normal and part of its look. Tight, distorted curling combined with stunted tops, though, usually signals a CO2 or micronutrient shortfall — this plant is an early warning sign for instability, so steady your CO2 and dosing and the new growth straightens out.

faqs.question: Can I grow Ammannia senegalensis without CO2?
faqs.answer: Realistically, no. It can limp along briefly, but without injected CO2 it loses color, sheds lower leaves, and gets outcompeted. If you want a similar copper-orange effect in a low-tech tank, this is not the plant — choose a hardier red stem instead.

propagation: Top the stems and replant cuttings; healthy bases branch out below the cut.
careSummary: Mint Charlie is a bright-green stem plant with small, rounded, mint-scented leaves that bring a fresh light-green tone to the midground. It is a little particular — it dislikes sudden changes and can melt when first introduced — but once settled in a stable tank with decent light and gentle CO2 it grows steadily into a soft, leafy clump.

faqs.question: Why did my Mint Charlie melt after I added it?
faqs.answer: It is sensitive to transition, and stems grown emersed often shed their original leaves when first submerged or when water parameters shift. Keep conditions stable, leave the stems planted, and watch for new growth from the tips and nodes — that signals it has adapted.

faqs.question: Does Mint Charlie really smell like mint?
faqs.answer: Yes — crush a leaf and you get a distinct minty aroma, which is why it is grown as a culinary and ornamental herb above water. In the aquarium the scent is irrelevant; people grow it for the cheerful light-green, small-leaved texture.

propagation: Top and replant the stems; the slow-growing bases branch out over time.
careSummary: Hygrophila 'Araguaia' is an unusually small, slow Hygrophila with narrow lance-shaped leaves that turn bronze, copper, and red-brown under strong light. Unlike the big, fast Temple Plant hygros, it stays compact and behaves like a delicate accent stem, which means it also needs more light and ideally CO2 to color up and grow densely rather than green and sparse.

faqs.question: How do I get the reddish-bronze color on Hygrophila Araguaia?
faqs.answer: High light is the main driver, with CO2 and good iron levels helping. Under medium-high light the narrow leaves develop coppery and red-brown tones, especially on the tops; in dimmer tanks it stays green. It is slow either way, so be patient as the color builds in new growth.

faqs.question: Is this the same as the larger Hygrophila Temple plants?
faqs.answer: No — despite sharing the genus, 'Araguaia' is far smaller, slower, and more demanding for color. Where Temple Plant is a fast, easy green hedge, Araguaia is a compact, light-hungry accent. Don't expect the same weed-like ease.

propagation: Top the stems and replant the cuttings; the bare bases regrow side shoots quickly.
careSummary: Moneywort is a hardy, undemanding stem plant with small, rounded, succulent light-green leaves along upright stalks — a tidy, brighter-green alternative to Bacopa caroliniana. It thrives in low-tech tanks across a wide range of water chemistry, asking only for decent light, and it holds its shape well between trims, which suits orderly Dutch-style rows.

faqs.question: How is Moneywort different from Bacopa caroliniana?
faqs.answer: Both are Bacopa, but Moneywort (B. monnieri) has smaller, lighter-green leaves and a more slender, faster habit, while caroliniana is chunkier with thicker, darker leaves and a faint lemon scent. Care is similar; the choice is about leaf size and color in your layout.

faqs.question: Why is my Moneywort dropping its lower leaves?
faqs.answer: Usually shading — the dense upper canopy starves the base of light. Trim the tops and replant them a little farther apart so light penetrates down each stem, and the new growth will stay leafy from top to bottom.

propagation: Top the stems and replant the cuttings; the bases branch from below the cut.
careSummary: Pogostemon erectus grows stiff, upright stems lined with fine needle-like leaves arranged in dense whorls, giving a crisp bottlebrush or conifer texture that stands out among softer plants. It needs strong light and ideally CO2 to stay compact and bright green; in lower light it grows thinner and stretches, losing the tight, structured look that makes it worth growing.

faqs.question: Why is my Pogostemon erectus growing thin and stretched?
faqs.answer: It is reaching for more light. This plant wants high light to keep its dense, bottlebrush form, and it grows sparse and leggy when underlit. Increase intensity, add CO2 if you can, and trim the leggy tops; the replanted cuttings come back fuller.

faqs.question: What does Pogostemon erectus pair well with?
faqs.answer: Its rigid, vertical, fine-needled texture contrasts beautifully with broad-leaved plants like swords and Anubias or rounded stems like Bacopa. Aquascapers often plant it in tight groups to read as a single structured column in the midground.

propagation: Spreads by runners; separate a mat into small plugs and plant them an inch or two apart to seed a lawn.
careSummary: Micro Sword forms a grassy foreground carpet of short, flat blades — broader and a touch more lush-looking than hairgrass — spreading by runners across the substrate. It carpets reliably but slowly, and it really wants medium-strong light reaching the floor plus CO2 and root feeding; in dim tanks it thins out and creeps along rather than filling in.

faqs.question: How long does Micro Sword take to carpet?
faqs.answer: It is a slow carpeter — expect a couple of months or more for full coverage even in good conditions, and longer without CO2. Plant small plugs in a grid to speed things along, keep the light reaching the substrate, and resist the urge to disturb it while runners establish.

faqs.question: Does Micro Sword need CO2?
faqs.answer: Not absolutely, but it makes a real difference. With CO2 and strong light it grows denser and carpets faster; in a low-tech tank it can survive and slowly spread, but it tends to stay sparse and is easily outcompeted by faster groundcovers.

propagation: Produces small daughter plants on short runners; separate them once established or let it form a slow, dense cluster.
careSummary: Cryptocoryne parva is the smallest of the crypts, forming tight little rosettes of narrow green leaves just a couple of inches tall — one of the few crypts usable as a true foreground plant. It is as undemanding chemically as any crypt but notably slow, so patience is essential; brighter light and CO2 keep it compact and help it knit into a low carpet over time.

faqs.question: Can I use Cryptocoryne parva as a foreground carpet?
faqs.answer: Yes, which is unusual for a crypt. It stays under about 3 inches and slowly clusters into a low mat. The catch is speed — it is one of the slowest aquarium plants, so plant several rosettes close together and expect months, not weeks, for coverage.

faqs.question: Does Crypt Parva melt like other crypts?
faqs.answer: It can, though usually less dramatically than larger crypts. After planting it may shed a few leaves while adjusting; leave the roots undisturbed and stable, and new adapted leaves grow in. Root tabs and patience are the main ingredients for success.
