propagation: Divides continuously at the surface; pull apart clusters by hand and skim off excess weekly once it gets going.
careSummary: Salvinia cucullata is a floating fern whose paired leaves fold upward into little cupped pockets, giving it a distinctive bead-like texture quite different from the flat, hairy Salvinia minima. It is a fast nutrient exporter that shades the tank and shelters fry, and like all Salvinia it is a regulated noxious weed in parts of the United States — check your local regulations and never release it into natural waterways.

faqs.question: How is Salvinia cucullata different from Salvinia minima?
faqs.answer: Cucullata's leaves curl upward into hooded, cup-shaped pockets, so a colony looks like a layer of little green beads, while minima stays flatter with bristly water-repellent hairs. Care is similar — both are fast floaters that hate surface splash — but cucullata's cupped form is the visual draw.

faqs.question: Why are my Salvinia leaves rotting or staying small?
faqs.answer: Surface agitation is the usual culprit — Salvinia hates having its leaf tops wetted by splash or strong flow, and waterlogged leaves brown and shrink. Corral it in a calm corner with airline tubing or a floating ring, and feed the water column lightly; healthy plants quickly grow larger, fuller cups.

faqs.question: Why are the leaves lying flat instead of cupping?
faqs.answer: The signature cups take time — young or freshly shipped plants often arrive flat and unremarkable. As the colony matures under strong light and the humid air trapped beneath a lid, each new leaf folds a little further upward into the hooded pocket shape. Flat leaves are a starting point, not a defect.

propagation: Divide a clump and re-tie the fragments to hardscape; trimmed pieces continue growing wherever they anchor.
careSummary: Willow Moss is a coldwater moss with larger, darker, keeled leaves arranged along trailing stems, giving a coarser, more cascading look than Java or Christmas moss. Its defining requirement is cool, well-oxygenated water with some flow — it struggles in warm tropical tanks above the upper 70s — which makes it an excellent choice for unheated, river-style, and coldwater setups.

faqs.question: Why is my Willow Moss melting in my tropical tank?
faqs.answer: It is almost certainly too warm. Fontinalis is a temperate, coldwater moss that prefers water in the 50s to low 70s Fahrenheit and tends to decline and brown in heated tropical tanks. If your tank runs warm, a Java or Christmas moss is a far better match.

faqs.question: How do I attach Willow Moss to wood or rock?
faqs.answer: Tie a thin layer down with thread or fishing line, or use a little gel superglue, the same as other mosses. It anchors with rhizoids over a few weeks. Give it gentle current — it grows fuller and greener in flowing, oxygen-rich water than in still corners.

propagation: Divide a patch and re-tie the fragments; trimmed cuttings grow wherever they are anchored.
careSummary: Weeping Moss is named for its drooping, teardrop-shaped fronds that hang downward, making it the go-to moss for creating a weeping-willow effect on tree-style hardscape and the undersides of branches. Care matches other mosses — low light, no CO2 required, tied to wood or stone — though a little CO2 and gentle flow encourage the dense, cascading growth it is prized for.

faqs.question: How do I get the weeping, cascading look?
faqs.answer: Attach a thin layer to the tops of branches or an overhanging structure and let gravity do the rest — the fronds naturally arch and droop downward as they grow. Light trims keep the curtain neat, and the effect strengthens as the moss thickens over a few months.

faqs.question: Why is the inside of my Weeping Moss clump turning brown?
faqs.answer: Thick clumps shade their own core, and the starved interior browns while the surface stays green. Trim the patch back periodically so light and flow reach the base, and keep layers thin when attaching; this keeps the whole pad green and growing evenly.

propagation: Spreads by runners; split a mat into small plugs and plant them close together to seed the carpet.
careSummary: Glossostigma is one of the smallest carpeting plants, forming a low lawn of tiny paired round leaves that hugs the substrate when conditions are right. It is genuinely demanding: it needs high light reaching the floor, pressurized CO2, and rich substrate, and without all three it grows upward and leggy instead of carpeting. Treat it as a high-tech plant, not a beginner groundcover.

faqs.question: Why is my Glossostigma growing tall instead of carpeting?
faqs.answer: Insufficient light is the classic cause. When underlit, glosso stretches vertically to reach the surface rather than creeping flat. It needs strong light hitting the substrate plus CO2 to grow horizontally; raise the light, keep CO2 steady, and trim it down to force low, dense growth.

faqs.question: Can a beginner grow a Glossostigma carpet?
faqs.answer: Honestly, it is a poor first carpet. It demands high light, CO2, and good substrate together, and falls apart if any are lacking. If you want a carpet without CO2, Monte Carlo or dwarf sagittaria are far more forgiving; save glosso for when you have a high-energy setup dialed in.

propagation: Spreads by creeping runners that root at the nodes; cut and replant any rooted section to start a new patch.
careSummary: Hydrocotyle tripartita is a fast, versatile creeper with small three-lobed, clover-like leaves on wiry stems that can be grown as a low foreground carpet, a midground bush, or trailing over hardscape. It is more forgiving than fine carpets like glosso — it spreads readily at medium light, faster with CO2 — but its vigor means frequent trimming to keep it from growing into a tangled mound.

faqs.question: How do I keep Hydrocotyle tripartita flat as a carpet?
faqs.answer: Trim it often and press loose runners back into the substrate. Left alone it piles up into a bushy mound and climbs over neighbors, so regular mowing and tucking is what keeps it a low, dense carpet. Brighter light also encourages flatter, more compact growth.

faqs.question: Does Hydrocotyle tripartita need CO2 to carpet?
faqs.answer: No, but CO2 speeds it up considerably and tightens the growth. At medium light without CO2 it still spreads — just more slowly and a bit looser. It is one of the easier carpeting options for a tank without injection, which is part of its popularity.

propagation: Mature bulbs slowly produce offset bulblets at the base; separate these once they have their own roots and leaves.
careSummary: Crinum calamistratum is a bulb plant that sends up long, narrow, deeply crinkled dark-green leaves that twist and sway like ribbons, making a striking low-tech centerpiece. It is hardy and undemanding — no CO2 needed and low light is fine — but it grows slowly and dislikes being moved, so plant the bulb once with root tabs nearby and leave it to settle in for the long term.

faqs.question: How deep do I plant a Crinum calamistratum bulb?
faqs.answer: Bury the roots and the lower portion of the bulb in the substrate but leave the top third or so of the bulb exposed — fully buried bulbs are prone to rot. Once it establishes roots it anchors firmly; place it where you want it permanently, since it resents being uprooted.

faqs.question: Why is my Crinum growing so slowly or losing a leaf after planting?
faqs.answer: Crinums are naturally slow and often sulk for a while after a move, sometimes dropping a leaf as they adjust. Don't dig the bulb up to check it — keep it planted, feed the roots with tabs, and be patient; an established Crinum is long-lived and steadily pushes out new ribbon leaves.

propagation: Cut healthy side shoots or top stems and replant the cuttings; the trimmed parent quickly throws new branches from the cut node.
careSummary: Hygrophila salicifolia is a fast, undemanding willow-leaf stem plant that grows in low to moderate light without CO2, making it a reliable nutrient sponge for new tanks. Under brighter light and a little fertilizer its narrow new growth takes on a faint blue-bronze flush, which is where the trade name 'Hygro Blue' comes from. Expect to prune it weekly once established, because it can easily reach the surface.

faqs.question: How is Hygrophila salicifolia different from regular Hygrophila corymbosa?
faqs.answer: Salicifolia has much narrower, willow-like leaves and a more delicate, feathery look, while corymbosa is broad-leaved and bushy. Both are equally easy, but salicifolia reads as a finer-textured background plant and groups well in a row of several stems.

faqs.question: Why are the lower leaves dropping off my Hygrophila?
faqs.answer: Bare lower stems usually mean the top growth is shading the bottom, or potassium and nitrogen are running low. Top and replant the healthy upper portions, thin the group so light reaches deeper, and add a balanced liquid fertilizer.

propagation: Trim the top few inches and replant; lateral shoots from the remaining stem can also be cut and rooted once they develop.
careSummary: Ludwigia ovalis is a rounder-leaved Ludwigia that shifts from green to coppery orange and pink as light intensity climbs, giving a Dutch layout a soft warm accent. It is more rewarding under strong light, CO2, and steady iron dosing; in dim tanks it stays green and the round leaf shape loses much of its appeal. Slightly cooler water often deepens the color.

faqs.question: How do I get the best pink and orange color from Ludwigia ovalis?
faqs.answer: High light is the single biggest factor, backed by CO2 and consistent iron-rich fertilization. Keeping nitrate on the lower side and water a touch cooler also pushes the reds and oranges; in soft light it simply stays green.

faqs.question: Is Ludwigia ovalis a fast or slow grower?
faqs.answer: It grows at a moderate pace, slower than stem weeds like Hygrophila, so it holds a shape longer between trims. That makes it well suited to a midground accent rather than a fast-filling background filler.

propagation: Cut and replant the tops; the parent stem branches readily at the cut, so each trim multiplies the group.
careSummary: Ludwigia 'Dark Red' is one of the easiest ways to get genuine red into a planted tank, holding deep burgundy tones even under moderate light where many red plants fade to green. CO2 and iron dosing make the color richer and the growth denser, but it is forgiving enough for beginners who want a low-effort red stem. Trim and replant regularly to keep a full, bushy group.

faqs.question: Will Ludwigia Dark Red stay red without CO2?
faqs.answer: Yes, more than most red plants. It keeps strong red coloration in moderate light without injected CO2, though CO2 and iron will intensify the hue and tighten the growth. This forgiving nature is what makes it a popular first red plant.

faqs.question: Why is my Ludwigia Dark Red growing tall and leggy?
faqs.answer: Stretching with wide gaps between leaves points to insufficient light reaching the plant. Raise the light or thin the planting, and top-and-replant the stems to encourage shorter, bushier regrowth.

propagation: Cut side shoots or top stems and replant; the parent branches at the cut node to build a fuller stand.
careSummary: Alternanthera reineckii 'Lilacina' is a broad-leaved cultivar of the popular red stem plant, showing a purplish-lilac underside that deepens with strong light and CO2. Like all A. reineckii it is happiest with good light, iron, and CO2, where it produces wide flat leaves of intense color; in low light it grows slowly and the underside violet fades. It is one of the most striking midground reds available.

faqs.question: How is 'Lilacina' different from regular Alternanthera reineckii?
faqs.answer: Lilacina has noticeably broader, more rounded leaves and a stronger purple-violet tint to the underside, whereas the standard form is narrower. Care is identical; Lilacina just reads as a bolder, more sculptural red plant.

faqs.question: Why are my Alternanthera reineckii leaves small and green instead of red?
faqs.answer: That almost always means not enough light, and often not enough iron or CO2. This is a high-light red plant; without those inputs it survives but stays compact and greenish rather than showing its full color.

propagation: Snip the tops and replant; it roots from almost any node and branches vigorously after each cut.
careSummary: Mexican Oak Leaf is a vigorous green stem plant named for its lobed, oak-like leaves, and it grows fast enough to outcompete algae in a newly set-up tank. It thrives in low to moderate light without CO2 and tolerates a wide temperature range, including cooler unheated tanks. Be ready to trim it frequently, since it can grow several inches a week and will sprawl across the surface if left alone.

faqs.question: Is Mexican Oak Leaf good for a beginner or a new tank?
faqs.answer: Yes. Its fast growth makes it an excellent early nutrient sponge that helps starve out algae while a tank cycles and stabilizes. The only catch is keeping up with trimming, because it grows quickly.

faqs.question: Why does my Mexican Oak Leaf grow sideways across the top of the tank?
faqs.answer: Once stems reach the surface they tend to bend and creep horizontally toward the light. Trim them well below the waterline and replant the cuttings upright to keep a tidy vertical background.

propagation: Cut the tops and replant the bushy cuttings; lateral shoots from the parent stem can also be separated and rooted.
careSummary: Green Myrio is a feathery stem plant whose finely divided whorls give a soft, cloud-like texture prized in Dutch and nature layouts. It grows fast and appreciates strong light, CO2, and steady micronutrients to keep the fine foliage dense rather than stringy. The lacy leaves are fragile, so plant it where flow and fish traffic are gentle.

faqs.question: Why is my Green Myrio thinning out or shedding its needles?
faqs.answer: Fine-leaved foxtails are sensitive to low CO2, low light, and nutrient swings, any of which cause the delicate whorls to thin or melt. Stabilize CO2, keep light strong, and dose micronutrients consistently for full, plush growth.

faqs.question: Does Green Myrio trap a lot of debris?
faqs.answer: Yes, its feathery foliage catches detritus and can collect algae if water movement is poor. A bit of gentle flow through the plant and regular trimming keep it clean and healthy.

propagation: Snap off any fragment and it will grow into a new plant; it self-propagates so readily it often spreads on its own.
careSummary: Guppy Grass is a brittle, fast-growing stem plant that can be planted, left to float, or wedged into hardscape, making it one of the most flexible nutrient exporters in the hobby. It needs no CO2 and very little light, and its dense tangle is a favorite spawning and fry-shelter plant for livebearers and egg-scatterers alike. It grows fast enough to need frequent thinning, and stray fragments will keep multiplying.

faqs.question: Why is Guppy Grass recommended for breeding tanks?
faqs.answer: Its fine, tangled growth gives newborn fry and scattered eggs dense cover from hungry adults, and it floats or anchors anywhere. Combined with fast nutrient uptake that keeps water clean, it is close to ideal for fry survival.

faqs.question: Is Guppy Grass hard to control?
faqs.answer: It can be. The plant is brittle and every broken fragment regrows, so it spreads quickly and you will be netting out excess regularly. Never release trimmings into local waterways.

propagation: Cut the rhizome into sections, each with several leaves and roots, and attach the divisions to new hardscape.
careSummary: Anubias hastifolia is one of the larger Anubias species, with distinctive arrowhead- or halberd-shaped leaves that lend an architectural look to bigger tanks. Like all Anubias it is nearly indestructible: low light, no CO2, no substrate needed, and the rhizome must stay above the substrate or it will rot. Its size and tough leaves make it a good background or hardscape centerpiece in jungle and biotope setups.

faqs.question: How big does Anubias hastifolia get?
faqs.answer: It is among the largest Anubias, with arrow-shaped leaves on long stalks that can reach well over a foot in a roomy tank. Plan for it as a background or feature plant rather than a small foreground piece.

faqs.question: Can I bury the rhizome of Anubias hastifolia in the substrate?
faqs.answer: No. Burying the thick rhizome causes it to rot. Tie or glue it to driftwood or rock with only the roots reaching into substrate, leaving the rhizome fully exposed.

propagation: Divide the rhizome into pieces with leaves and roots attached, and fasten each section to wood or stone.
careSummary: Anubias afzelii is a tall, slender Anubias with long lance-shaped leaves, giving a more upright silhouette than the rounder barteri types. It shares the genus's bulletproof care: thrives in low light without CO2, tolerates a wide range of water, and must have its rhizome kept above the substrate. The narrower leaves make it a graceful background or driftwood plant that stays clean of algae in shaded spots.

faqs.question: How is Anubias afzelii different from Anubias barteri?
faqs.answer: Afzelii has longer, narrower, more lance-shaped leaves and a taller habit, while barteri leaves are broader and rounder. Care is the same, but afzelii suits a slimmer, more vertical look in the midground or background.

faqs.question: Why does my Anubias afzelii grow so slowly?
faqs.answer: Slow growth is normal for the entire genus and not a problem. It puts out only a few new leaves per month; the trade-off is that it needs almost no maintenance and rarely outgrows its spot.

scientificName: Anubias barteri var. nana x Anubias gilletii
propagation: Split the rhizome into sections with roots and leaves, then attach each piece to hardscape.
careSummary: Anubias 'Nangi' is a hybrid with elongated, pointed leaves and a compact, mid-sized habit that sits between the tiny nana and the larger species. It offers the same effortless care as the rest of the genus: low light, no CO2, rhizome kept above the substrate, and near-total immunity to being eaten. Its modest size and elegant leaf shape make it a versatile midground or driftwood accent.

faqs.question: Is Anubias Nangi a good size for a smaller tank?
faqs.answer: Yes. It stays more compact than the big species while having more presence than nana petite, making it a good fit for the midground of a 10 to 29 gallon tank. It grows slowly and won't crowd neighbors.

faqs.question: Will Anubias Nangi attach to driftwood on its own?
faqs.answer: It will, given time. Glue or tie the rhizome to wood or rock and within a couple of months its roots grip the surface so the plant holds on permanently. Keep the rhizome itself exposed.

propagation: Divide the creeping rhizome into sections, each with roots and a growth point, much like splitting a crypt.
careSummary: Lagenandra meeboldii 'Red' is a crypt relative with thick, leathery leaves that flush reddish-pink to copper, offering a slow, low-light alternative to demanding red stem plants. It grows from a creeping rhizome and can be planted in substrate or attached to hardscape, asking only for stable conditions and patience. Like crypts, it may melt when first moved and then regrow acclimated leaves.

faqs.question: Why did my Lagenandra lose all its leaves after planting?
faqs.answer: Lagenandra, like Cryptocoryne, often melts back when adjusting to new water, dropping leaves before regrowing. Leave the rhizome undisturbed and stable, and new acclimated foliage will emerge over the following weeks.

faqs.question: Does Lagenandra meeboldii need CO2 to stay red?
faqs.answer: It does not require CO2 and keeps reddish tones in good low-tech tanks, but stronger light, CO2, and iron deepen the color. It is far more forgiving than red stems, trading speed for reliability.

propagation: Divide the rhizome, or replant the plantlets that sprout along leaf edges and tips.
careSummary: Java Fern 'Trident' is a finely branched cultivar whose narrow leaves split into multiple finger-like lobes, giving a softer, more fern-like texture than the standard plant. It keeps all the toughness of regular Java Fern: low light, no CO2, no substrate, and a rhizome that must stay tied to wood or rock rather than buried. The delicate, bushy fronds make it a popular choice for adding fine detail to driftwood.

faqs.question: How is 'Trident' different from regular Java Fern?
faqs.answer: Trident leaves are narrower and split into several finger-like segments, creating a lacy, branched look, whereas standard Java Fern has simple broad blades. The care is identical; Trident just offers a finer, more textured appearance.

faqs.question: Why are there little plantlets growing on my Java Fern Trident leaves?
faqs.answer: That is normal vegetative reproduction. The parent grows tiny daughter plants along the leaf edges and tips; once they have a few leaves and roots you can detach them and attach them to new hardscape.

propagation: Detach the daughter plants that grow on flower stalks (adventitious shoots) once they have their own roots and leaves.
careSummary: Red Rubin Sword is a large rosette plant whose broad lance-shaped leaves emerge deep wine-red and mature to a reddish-bronze, making it a striking centerpiece for bigger tanks. It is rooted-feeding, so it benefits most from nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs, plus moderate light to bring out the color. Give it room: a mature plant easily spans a foot across and dominates the background.

faqs.question: How do I feed a Red Rubin Sword?
faqs.answer: Swords are heavy root feeders, so push a couple of root tabs into the substrate near the base every few months. Rich substrate and root feeding matter more than liquid dosing for full, well-colored leaves.

faqs.question: My Red Rubin Sword's new leaves are red but old ones turn green. Is that a problem?
faqs.answer: No, that is normal. New growth emerges most intensely red and gradually mellows to bronze-green as it ages. Brighter light keeps more leaves colored, but the fade on older foliage is expected.

propagation: Daughter plants form on the long flower stalks; pot them up once they have several leaves and roots.
careSummary: Red Ozelot Sword is prized for its dramatic foliage: reddish-brown leaves freckled with darker maroon spots, like an animal print, that hold up well even in moderate light. It is an undemanding rooted plant that thrives on nutrient-rich substrate and root tabs, growing into a large background specimen. The spotting is most vivid on new leaves and under brighter light.

faqs.question: Why doesn't my Red Ozelot Sword have many spots?
faqs.answer: The maroon spotting develops most strongly on new growth under good light. Low light produces greener, less freckled leaves; increase lighting and feed the roots well to bring out the classic ozelot pattern.

faqs.question: How big does a Red Ozelot Sword get and where should I plant it?
faqs.answer: It becomes a sizeable rosette around a foot and a half tall and wide, so plant it as a background centerpiece in a 29 gallon tank or larger. In small tanks it quickly outgrows the space.

propagation: Separate the adventitious daughter plants that appear on flower stalks once they have rooted.
careSummary: Kleiner Bar is a compact, narrow-leaved sword whose new growth flushes red and copper, staying smaller than the big Amazon-type swords so it fits midground positions in moderate tanks. It is a rooted feeder that does best with root tabs and a rich substrate, and the reds intensify under stronger light. Its restrained size makes it one of the more versatile red swords for everyday aquascapes.

faqs.question: Is Kleiner Bar small enough for a midground spot?
faqs.answer: Relatively, yes. It stays around a foot tall with narrow leaves, noticeably more compact than Amazon or Red Rubin swords, so it can sit in the midground of a medium tank rather than only the back.

faqs.question: How do I keep the red color in a Kleiner Bar Sword?
faqs.answer: Provide moderate to strong light and feed the roots with tabs in a nutrient-rich substrate. New leaves emerge reddest; brighter light keeps more of the plant colored before leaves age to green-bronze.

propagation: It spreads by runners; daughter plants pop up on horizontal shoots and can be cut free once rooted.
careSummary: Helanthium 'Quadricostatus' is a small chain-sword that sends out runners to form a bright green grassy carpet in the foreground or midground. It is easier than fine carpets like dwarf baby tears, spreading reliably in moderate light, though CO2 and root feeding make it denser and faster. Trim runners to keep it from wandering into neighboring plantings.

faqs.question: Does Helanthium Quadricostatus need CO2 to carpet?
faqs.answer: No. It will spread by runners in moderate light without CO2, which makes it a forgiving carpeting option for low-tech tanks. CO2 and root tabs simply produce a thicker, faster carpet.

faqs.question: How is this different from a regular chain sword?
faqs.answer: It is one of the chain swords, with slightly broader strap-like leaves than the narrowest tenellus forms and a quick runner habit. It stays low, around four to six inches, and fills space horizontally rather than growing tall.

propagation: Separate the daughter plants that emerge on runners from the parent rhizome once they have roots.
careSummary: Cryptocoryne 'Pink Flamingo' is a striking pink-to-magenta crypt that, unlike most easy Cryptocoryne, genuinely needs high light, CO2, and rich nutrients to hold its color and grow well. It is slow and can be temperamental, prone to melting and to reverting toward green if conditions slip. Treat it as a demanding showpiece rather than a beginner crypt.

faqs.question: Why is my Pink Flamingo crypt turning green?
faqs.answer: This cultivar keeps its pink only under strong light and CO2 with good iron levels. In dimmer or CO2-free tanks it survives but the new leaves come in greener, losing the signature flamingo color.

faqs.question: Is Cryptocoryne Pink Flamingo really harder than other crypts?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most crypts are easy low-light plants, but Pink Flamingo demands high light, injected CO2, and steady fertilization to thrive and stay pink. It is one of the few crypts genuinely rated for advanced keepers.

propagation: It produces side shoots and runners from the base; separate the new rosettes once they root, or cut and replant tops.
careSummary: Pogostemon helferi, known as Downoi, forms low star-shaped rosettes of crinkled, wavy-edged leaves that make an unmistakable foreground accent. It does best with strong light, CO2, and good calcium and micronutrient levels, which keep the curly leaves compact and the clumps spreading. Without enough light or CO2 it grows tall and loses the tight, crinkled form that makes it special.

faqs.question: Why is my Downoi growing tall and leggy instead of staying low?
faqs.answer: Stretching means it needs more light, and usually CO2. Under high light with CO2 it stays low and crinkled; in dim tanks it reaches upward and loses its compact star shape.

faqs.question: Does Pogostemon helferi need anything special in the water?
faqs.answer: It responds strongly to adequate calcium and a full micronutrient mix, in addition to CO2 and bright light. Deficiencies show as stunted or pale new rosettes, so steady fertilization keeps it healthy.

propagation: Grows from a bulb; mature plants may produce seed on flower spikes, and large bulbs can sometimes be divided.
careSummary: Aponogeton ulvaceus grows from a bulb into a fountain of translucent, ruffled light-green leaves that can fill the back of a large tank quickly. It is a fast grower given good light and nutrient-rich substrate, but it is a true bulb plant that benefits from a rest period each year when it naturally dies back. Plant the bulb only partway into the substrate so the crown stays exposed.

faqs.question: My Aponogeton ulvaceus suddenly lost all its leaves. Is it dead?
faqs.answer: Probably not. Aponogeton bulbs go through dormancy and can drop their foliage; leave the firm bulb in place and it usually resprouts after a few weeks of rest. A bulb that has gone soft and mushy, however, has rotted.

faqs.question: How deep should I plant an Aponogeton bulb?
faqs.answer: Bury only the lower half to two-thirds, leaving the growing tip and crown above the substrate. Burying the whole bulb often causes it to rot before it sprouts.

propagation: Grows from a bulb and can set seed from flower spikes when conditions are right; bulbs are not easily divided.
careSummary: Aponogeton boivinianus is a Madagascar bulb plant whose dark green leaves carry a distinctive deeply textured, embossed surface, making it a dramatic background specimen. It is more demanding than the common Aponogetons, wanting strong light, rich substrate, and good nutrients, and like its relatives it needs an annual dormant period. Plant the bulb partway in and be patient, as it can sulk before establishing.

faqs.question: Why does Aponogeton boivinianus need a dormancy period?
faqs.answer: It is a seasonal bulb plant from Madagascar that naturally rests each year. After a strong growth phase it may die back; lift the firm bulb into cooler storage for a few weeks if it stalls, then replant to trigger fresh growth.

faqs.question: What makes the leaves of this plant look bumpy?
faqs.answer: That embossed, quilted texture between the leaf veins is the species' natural feature, not damage or deficiency. Strong light and good nutrition make the dark green, textured leaves most pronounced.

propagation: Grows from a bulb and forms daughter bulblets at the base; it also sends up surface pads that can be pruned to keep it submerged.
careSummary: The Dwarf Lily grows from a bulb into a rosette of arrow-shaped leaves that range from green to deep red and bronze depending on light. It is an easy, fast bulb plant that quickly becomes a colorful centerpiece, but it will rush to send floating lily pads to the surface; trim those pad stems at the base to keep it growing as a submerged bush. Plant the bulb with its growing tip exposed.

faqs.question: How do I stop my Dwarf Lily from sending leaves to the surface?
faqs.answer: It naturally wants to put up floating pads. Snip each pad stem off at the base as it appears, and the plant will keep producing submerged leaves instead, staying a compact underwater bush.

faqs.question: Why is my Dwarf Lily green instead of red?
faqs.answer: Leaf color depends heavily on light intensity. Under stronger light the arrowhead leaves develop red and bronze tones, while in lower light they stay green. CO2 and nutrients also help bring out color.

propagation: Divide a clump and tie or glue the pieces to new hardscape; each fragment grows into a fresh patch.
careSummary: Flame Moss is unusual among aquatic mosses for growing upward in twisting, flame-like vertical columns rather than spreading flat, giving a tank a fiery, textured look. It is easy and low-demand, tolerating low light and no CO2, though brighter light and CO2 keep the upward growth tighter and greener. Attach it to wood or rock and trim the tips to maintain the flame shape.

faqs.question: What makes Flame Moss grow upward like flames?
faqs.answer: It is the species' natural growth habit: the fronds twist and grow vertically rather than creeping horizontally like Java or Christmas moss. Good light and gentle flow encourage the tightest, most flame-like columns.

faqs.question: How do I attach Flame Moss to driftwood?
faqs.answer: Spread a thin layer over the wood or rock and secure it with thread, fishing line, or small dabs of cyanoacrylate gel glue. Within a few weeks its rhizoids grip the surface and the thread can be left to dissolve or removed.

propagation: Split the mat and re-tie the fragments to hardscape, where each piece spreads into a new patch.
careSummary: Peacock Moss is named for the iridescent, fan-shaped fronds that fan out in overlapping tiers resembling peacock feathers, especially attractive under good light. It is an easy low-light moss that needs no CO2, though brighter light and CO2 tighten the fronds and bring out the characteristic teal sheen. Attach it to wood or rock and give it gentle flow to keep debris from settling in the dense growth.

faqs.question: How is Peacock Moss different from Christmas or Java moss?
faqs.answer: Peacock Moss grows in flat, fanning tiers with a distinctive feather-like, slightly iridescent pattern, whereas Java moss is stringy and Christmas moss branches in tighter triangular fronds. Peacock's layered fans give a more ornamental, structured texture.

faqs.question: Why does my Peacock Moss collect debris and look brown?
faqs.answer: Its dense, layered fronds trap detritus, which can smother and brown the lower growth. Provide gentle water movement across it and give it an occasional turkey-baster blast to keep it clean and green.

propagation: It multiplies by fragmentation; the floating fronds branch and split into new plants on their own.
careSummary: Salvinia natans is a floating fern with paired oval leaves covered in tiny water-repellent hairs that keep it riding high and dry on the surface, shading the tank and exporting nutrients fast. It needs no CO2 and thrives across a wide light range, growing quickly enough to need regular thinning. Because Salvinia species are regulated as noxious weeds in parts of the United States, check your state and local rules and never release it into natural waterways.

faqs.question: Is Salvinia natans legal to own in the United States?
faqs.answer: It depends on your state. The genus Salvinia includes federally regulated noxious weeds, and several states restrict or ban Salvinia species, so confirm your local regulations before buying and dispose of trimmings in the trash, never outdoors.

faqs.question: Why are my Salvinia leaves turning brown or sinking?
faqs.answer: Usually it is overhead condensation or water droplets defeating the leaf's water-repellent hairs, often from a tight lid or splashy filter return. Improve airflow above the surface and avoid wetting the leaves, and dose a little fertilizer if growth is also pale.

propagation: Each frond buds off new fronds continuously, so it doubles its surface coverage rapidly without any intervention.
careSummary: Giant Duckweed is the larger cousin of common duckweed, with rounder fronds up to about a quarter inch across and several trailing roots beneath each, making it easier to net out than tiny Lemna. It is an extremely fast floating nutrient sponge that shades the tank and helps fight algae, needing no CO2 and almost any light. The trade-off is its speed: it can blanket the surface in days and is very hard to fully remove once introduced.

faqs.question: Is Giant Duckweed easier to control than regular duckweed?
faqs.answer: Somewhat. Its larger fronds and multiple roots make it easier to scoop out by hand or net than the tiny single-rooted common duckweed, but it still grows explosively and a few leftover fronds will repopulate the surface.

faqs.question: Will Giant Duckweed block light from my other plants?
faqs.answer: It can. A thick surface mat shades plants below and may starve light-hungry species, so skim off a portion regularly to keep coverage partial. Many keepers run it deliberately in low-light or betta tanks where the shade is welcome.

propagation: Divide the thallus by hand — every fragment keeps growing, floating or re-tied to stone.
careSummary: Riccia is technically a liverwort, and it leads a double life: left alone it floats as a tangle of bright forked ribbons, but tied over flat stones under strong light and CO2 it becomes the famous pearling 'Riccia stone' carpet that Takashi Amano built nature aquascaping around. It never anchors itself, so the carpet look is a commitment — mesh, weekly trims, and re-tying are part of the deal.

faqs.question: Why does my Riccia keep floating away even though I tied it down?
faqs.answer: Two culprits: untrimmed growth shades the lower layer until it dies and lets go, and oxygen bubbles from heavy pearling build up under the mat and lift it. Trim weekly, press the mat gently to release trapped gas, and refresh the mesh or thread every month or two — Riccia has no roots and never attaches on its own.

faqs.question: Can Riccia carpet without CO2?
faqs.answer: Realistically no — without injected CO2 it grows loose and stringy and detaches rather than forming the dense anchored cushion. It is still an excellent low-tech plant, just as a floater, where it gets all the CO2 it needs from the air and provides great fry and shrimp cover.

propagation: Top the stems and replant the cuttings; keep spacing generous so light reaches every stem.
careSummary: Rotala macrandra is the hobby's reference red stem — broad leaves in saturated red to magenta that no easier plant matches. It is honestly demanding: high light, injected CO2, iron-rich fertilization, and above all stability, because it melts faster than almost anything else when CO2 or nutrients swing. A showpiece for Dutch-style tanks and aquascapers who already have their parameters dialed in.

faqs.question: Why is my Rotala macrandra losing its red color?
faqs.answer: Color collapse comes from whichever of its three pillars slipped: light intensity, iron, or CO2. All three need to be high and steady — under strong light with iron-rich dosing and stable CO2 the tops glow magenta; lose any one and new growth goes pale pink to green within a week or two.

faqs.question: Why are the stems melting from the bottom up?
faqs.answer: Parameter instability is the usual cause — fluctuating CO2 is the classic trigger, followed by dense planting that shades the lower stems. Keep CO2 rock-steady, thin the bush so light reaches the base, and replant healthy tops rather than nursing damaged stems.
